rationale: I split Brain & Cognitive Sciences into 14 practitioner-facing subspecialties to balance coverage and pickability: enough granularity to separate experimental, clinical and computational work, but not so many that the grid becomes noisy. I kept clinical and psychiatric cognitive work in the same top-level while routing pharmacologic drug‑treatment trials to Medicine (per instructions). I deliberately separated computational/modeling fields from behavioral and imaging fields because practitioners self-identify with those labels, and I split cognition-related specialties (e.g., Language, Decision, Learning) rather than lumping them into a single “Cognition” card to improve discoverability. I avoided hyper-specific disease or molecular categories — those belong to Medicine or Biology when primarily cellular/biochemical.

subspecialties.name: Systems Neuroscience
subspecialties.tagline: Network, circuit, and systems-level brain function
covers: Population and circuit recordings, optogenetics, electrophysiology
Examples: cortical circuit dynamics, thalamocortical loops, hippocampal network oscillations
explicitly_excludes: Cellular/molecular synaptic mechanisms — goes to Biology
Pure behavioral-only psychology studies — goes to Cognitive Psychology

subspecialties.name: Cognitive Neuroscience
subspecialties.tagline: Neural basis of perception, memory, attention and higher cognition
covers: Human and animal studies linking brain activity to cognitive functions
Examples: fMRI/EEG studies of attention, neural correlates of working memory
explicitly_excludes: Method development in imaging hardware/algorithms — goes to Neuroimaging & Neurotechnology
Pure behavioral theory without neural measures — goes to Cognitive Psychology

subspecialties.name: Computational Neuroscience
subspecialties.tagline: Biophysically grounded models of neurons, circuits, and networks
covers: Spiking neuron models, conductance-based modeling, synaptic plasticity theory
Examples: models of sensory coding, large-scale brain simulations
explicitly_excludes: Abstract cognitive-level modeling not tied to neural plausibility — goes to Computational Cognitive Science
Machine learning methods papers without neural focus — goes to Machine Learning under other categories

subspecialties.name: Cognitive Psychology
subspecialties.tagline: Behavioral experiments and theory of human cognition
covers: Reaction-time, psychophysics, experimental studies of memory, attention, perception
Examples: dual-task paradigms, priming studies, behavioral models of memory
explicitly-excludes: Clinical assessments and patient studies of cognitive deficits — goes to Neuropsychology
Neural imaging correlates without behavioral focus — goes to Cognitive Neuroscience

subspecialties.name: Computational Cognitive Science
subspecialties.tagline: Cognitive models, AI-level theories, and cognitive architectures
covers: Symbolic and connectionist cognitive models, Bayesian cognitive modeling, cognitive architectures
Examples: models of reasoning, language understanding, cognitive-level reinforcement learning
explicitly-excludes: Neural-data-grounded computational models — goes to Computational Neuroscience
Engineering applications of ML (NLP/vision) with little cognitive theory — goes to Machine Learning elsewhere

subspecialties.name: Neuroimaging & Neurotechnology
subspecialties.tagline: Imaging, recording tools, and brain–machine interfaces
covers: fMRI/EEG/MEG methods, invasive recording technology, brain–computer interface hardware
Examples: high-field fMRI methods, novel electrode arrays, decoding pipelines
explicitly-excludes: Use of imaging purely to test cognitive hypotheses (analysis belongs to Cognitive Neuroscience)
Device clinical trials focused on safety/regulatory efficacy — goes to Medicine

subspecialties.name: Neuropsychology
subspecialties.tagline: Cognitive assessment and brain–behavior syndromes after injury
covers: Clinical and experimental studies of cognitive deficits, lesion mapping, rehabilitation
Examples: stroke aphasia, TBI cognitive trajectories, neuropsychological testing
explicitly-excludes: Primary pharmacologic treatment trials for psychiatric/neuro conditions — goes to Medicine
Basic developmental cognitive studies in healthy populations — goes to Developmental Psychology

subspecialties.name: Behavioral Neuroscience
subspecialties.tagline: Animal behavior and neural mechanisms of behavior
covers: Rodent/animal behavioral paradigms tied to neural circuits and behavior
Examples: fear conditioning neural mechanisms, ethology-linked neural recording
explicitly-excludes: Human behavioral studies without neural/animal link — goes to Cognitive Psychology
Molecular/cellular studies of behavior mechanisms — goes to Biology

subspecialties.name: Developmental Psychology
subspecialties.tagline: Cognitive and behavioral development across the lifespan
covers: Child development, adolescent cognition, lifespan cognitive changes, developmental disorders
Examples: language acquisition studies, developmental trajectories of executive function
explicitly-excludes: Basic developmental neuroscience at the cellular level — goes to Biology
Clinical pediatric neurology focused on treatment — goes to Medicine

subspecialties.name: Psychiatry
subspecialties.tagline: Clinical studies of mental disorders and cognitive/behavioral syndromes
covers: Clinical and cognitive studies of psychiatric conditions, neurobiological correlates, diagnostic research
Examples: cognitive deficits in depression, neuroimaging in schizophrenia
explicitly-excludes: Drug trials and pharmacotherapy-focused clinical trials — goes to Medicine
Pure psychological therapy method trials without psychiatric diagnosis — goes to Clinical Psychology elsewhere

subspecialties.name: Language & Cognition
subspecialties.tagline: Neurolinguistics, psycholinguistics, and language processing research
covers: Language acquisition, processing, neural bases of language, aphasia studies
Examples: sentence processing EEG/fMRI, bilingual cognition, speech perception
explicitly-excludes: Machine learning / NLP engineering papers without cognitive focus — goes to Machine Learning
Pure computational linguistics without cognitive grounding — goes to Computational Linguistics elsewhere

subspecialties.name: Sensation & Perception
subspecialties.tagline: Sensory systems, psychophysics, and perceptual processes
covers: Vision, audition, somatosensation studies in humans and animals; psychophysical methods
Examples: perceptual thresholds, multisensory integration, sensory adaptation
explicitly-excludes: Higher-level cognitive tasks (e.g., memory/decision) without sensory focus — goes to Cognitive Psychology
Cellular sensory transduction mechanisms — goes to Biology

subspecialties.name: Learning & Memory
subspecialties.tagline: Mechanisms and models of learning and memory
covers: Behavioral, neural, and computational studies of plasticity, memory formation, consolidation
Examples: synaptic plasticity studies with behavioral readouts, memory systems research
explicitly-excludes: Molecular synaptic mechanism-focused work without behavior — goes to Biology
Applied educational intervention trials — goes to Education/Clinical Psychology

subspecialties.name: Decision Neuroscience
subspecialties.tagline: Neural and behavioral bases of decision-making and value
covers: Neuroeconomics, reward and motivation studies, models of choice behavior
Examples: reinforcement learning in humans/animals, neural basis of risk-taking
explicitly-excludes: Pure economic theory without behavioral/neural data — goes to Economics
Applied decision‑engineering ML work without cognitive framing — goes to Machine Learning

uncovered_concerns: Where to put applied AI/NLP papers: if primarily engineering or dataset/model work they belong to Machine Learning/CS; if they address cognitive mechanisms/psycholinguistics they appear under Computational Cognitive Science or Language & Cognition.

Psychopharmacology and drug trials: these often straddle Psychiatry and Medicine; per onboarding rules, pharmacologic treatment trials are routed to Medicine, while mechanistic psychiatric/neurobiological studies without clinical drug trials stay in Psychiatry or Clinical Neuroscience.

Overlap between Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging: imaging methods papers that introduce new neurotech go to Neuroimaging & Neurotechnology, whereas studies using those methods to answer cognition questions go to Cognitive Neuroscience.

Developmental cognitive neuroscience: could reasonably map to Developmental Psychology or Cognitive Neuroscience depending on whether emphasis is behavioral/developmental vs. neural-mechanistic; we expect authors to pick the primary framing.
